title: Near surface donor-acceptor pairs in hydrogenated homoepitaxial diamond nanolayers
authors: A. M. Romshin, A. P. Bolshakov, A. A. Zhivopistsev, A. V. Gritsienko, O. S. Kudryavtsev, P. A. Pivovarov, V. G. Ralchenko, I. I. Vlasov
abstract: Hydrogen-terminated diamond is known for its p-type surface conductivity, which arises from a near-surface hole accumulation layer induced by adsorbed acceptor species. Here, we demonstrate that these surface acceptors also form optically active donor-acceptor pairs (DAP) with substitutional nitrogen donors in diamond. The insertion of a nominally undoped CVD interlayer between a nitrogen-rich HPHT substrate and a hydrogen-terminated surface enables the precise tuning of the donor-acceptor separation with nanometer precision. Radiative DAP recombination appears as bright, spectrally narrow lines whose intensity, energy, and decay dynamics depend systematically on interlayer thickness. Individual lines show single-photon statistics, while ensembles exhibit strong polarization anisotropy reflecting the planar donor-acceptor geometry. These findings reveal an optical counterpart of hydrogen-induced surface transfer doping in diamond and establish a surface-defined, nanometer-tunable platform for engineering DAP-based quantum emitters.
summary: This paper demonstrates the ability to engineer and optically probe donor-acceptor pairs (DAPs) in diamond nanolayers by controlling the separation distance between the donor and acceptor sites using an intermediate CVD layer. By measuring the PL intensity and lifetime as a function of this separation, the authors confirm that DAP recombination is the source of the narrowband emission, establishing a tunable platform for quantum emitters.
main_problem: The study investigates the optical properties of donor-acceptor pairs (DAPs) formed between near-surface acceptor species and substitutional nitrogen donors in hydrogenated diamond nanolayers.
main_result: The PL intensity and lifetime exhibit a non-monotonic dependence on the intermediate CVD layer thickness, peaking at a characteristic separation distance of approximately 1.7 nm.
method: The research combines confocal photoluminescence (PL) microspectroscopy, time-resolved measurements, and electrostatic force microscopy (EFM) mapping.
model_or_system: The physical system is hydrogenated homoepitaxial diamond nanolayers, where the donor-acceptor separation distance ($d$) is precisely tuned by inserting a nominally undoped CVD interlayer. The recombination process is modeled using a quantum yield ($\eta(d)$) derived from radiative and non-radiative rates.
key_observables: Bright, spectrally narrow PL lines (545-650 nm), PL intensity vs. thickness, PL lifetime ($	au_{mean}$), and EFM phase contrast changes.
important_parameters: Donor-acceptor separation distance ($d$), which is tunable via the CVD layer thickness (optimal range 1.5-2.5 nm).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the hydrogen-like model for DAP recombination and assumes the PL intensity is a product of the quantum yield and an energy matching function.
figures_summary: Figures show PL intensity maps correlating with topography, and PL spectra demonstrating the dependence of emission on nanolayer thickness, confirming the maximum intensity in the 1.5-2.5 nm range.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the physical system (H-terminated diamond on N-doped substrate), details the theoretical model linking PL to separation distance, presents measurements showing thickness dependence, and concludes by confirming DAP recombination as the source of narrowband emission.
why_interesting: This work provides a highly controllable, solid-state platform for engineering quantum emitters whose optical properties (emission energy, intensity, lifetime) can be tuned by controlling the physical separation between two localized electronic states, which is a key concept in quantum optics and AMO physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
